items.q: What is the difference between primary and secondary data in geography fieldwork?
items.correct: Primary data is collected first-hand by the researcher (surveys, measurements, observations); secondary data already exists and is found in sources (census data, maps, published reports).
items.misconception: Primary data is more important and secondary data is less reliable.
items.random_wrong: Primary data is collected in primary schools and secondary data in secondary schools.
items.why: It's about source, not quality. Secondary data (e.g. Census) is often more reliable than small primary surveys. Good fieldwork triangulates both — the distinction is who collected it, not how useful it is.

items.q: What is the difference between qualitative and quantitative data?
items.correct: Quantitative data is numerical and measurable (traffic counts, river width); qualitative data is descriptive and non-numerical (interview quotes, photo descriptions, field sketches).
items.misconception: Quantitative data is always more scientific and reliable than qualitative.
items.random_wrong: Qualitative data means high-quality data and quantitative means a lot of data.
items.why: Both have value: numbers show patterns; words explain why. Mixed-methods studies combine them. Students wrongly assume numbers are objective and words are subjective — both can be done well or poorly.

items.q: What is the difference between random, stratified and systematic sampling?
items.correct: Random: every member has equal chance of selection. Systematic: pick every nth item. Stratified: divide the population into groups and sample proportionally from each — ensures subgroup representation.
items.misconception: All three sampling methods produce the same results.
items.random_wrong: Stratified sampling means picking samples from different strata of rock.
items.why: Stratified sampling is for ensuring subgroups (e.g. age groups, land-use types) are proportionately represented. The choice depends on the population structure — wrong choice gives biased results.

items.q: Which test compares observed vs expected frequencies?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Chi-squared. “Spearman's” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Correct answer: Alonso. “Burgess” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Common significance level for hypothesis testing?
items.why: Exact answer: 0.05 p. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
